title: Undo as a Tree
subtitle: {key:g}{key:-} and {key:g}{key:+} walk by time. `:undolist` shows the branches.
summary: Vim's undo isn't a stack — it's a tree. Every undo branch is preserved. {key:g}{key:-} and {key:g}{key:+} walk by chronological time; `:undolist` shows all branches; `:earlier`/`:later` jump by time intervals.

blocks.text: In most editors, undo is a stack: redo a mistake, the original is gone forever. In Vim, undo is a **tree** — every branch is kept.

rows: Go to OLDER state by chronological time
Go to NEWER state by chronological time

blocks.title: The Tree Structure
blocks.text:
Each *change* (one Insert session, one delete, one substitute, etc.) creates a new **node** in the undo tree. {key:u} walks toward the parent of the current node; {key:Ctrl-R} walks back toward the most recent child.

When you edit, undo, then **edit again**, the new edit becomes a *new branch* — and the previous redo path is preserved as a sibling. Without {key:g}{key:-}/{key:g}{key:+}, you'd never reach that sibling again.

  • {key:g}{key:-} / {key:g}{key:+} walk **by time** — they ignore the tree shape and visit nodes in chronological creation order. This lets you reach any state regardless of branching.
  • `:undolist` shows the tree as a table: number, time, save count, branch info. Use `:undo N` to jump to a specific node.
  • `:earlier 5m` and `:later 30s` jump by wall-clock time — *priceless* when you realize you broke something a few minutes ago.

The undo tree is persisted across sessions if you `:set undofile` — Vim writes a `.<filename>.un~` file beside each saved file. Real time-travel.
Plugins like *undotree* visualize the tree as a sidebar. The data is all there in `:undolist` — the plugin just makes it readable.

blocks.title: Undo is a tree, not a stack
blocks.text: Each undo unit creates a node in a directed graph. Linear edits build a chain; an edit after an undo creates a branch. {key:u}/{key:Ctrl-R} walk one chain; {key:g}{key:-}/{key:g}{key:+} walk by chronological time across branches; `:earlier`/`:later` walk by wall-clock time. Most editors throw away post-undo edits — Vim preserves them as branches you can navigate back to.
